[00:56] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Content Distribution Strategy - Webinar Targeting"
NÚMEROS: 72 horas, 10-15,000 registrantes, $80,000 investimento mensal
VISUAL: Estrutura de funil de webinar, linha do tempo de 72 horas destacada, fluxo de registrantes para webinar para follow-up
[01:12] Okay, so first of all, uh, just a reminder on why we do this. Okay. So, number one, this particular content sequence that I'm talking about here is targeting on a monthly basis a list of webinar registrants over a 72-hour duration of time from the webinar start date to two days after the webinar.
[01:32] Um, to be clear, allow me to explain this literally and then I'll explain like, you know, how we do this same thing for other funnel types as well, just so you guys can understand like where we're doing it first and then we'll go through the content.
[01:44] So, number one, this particular client, um, she does a monthly webinar right in the middle of the month, typically on like the 13th or the 15th. Um, to be clear, that monthly webinar brings in about 10 to 15,000 total registrants. Um, I spend on the paid ad side about $80,000 monthly on generating those webinar registrants.
[02:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Hammer Them Strategy / Mini Tornado"
NÚMEROS: 10-15,000 registrantes, $80,000 ad spend mensal, 72 horas de targeting
VISUAL: Conceito de "martelo" visual, tornado de conteúdo cercando o cliente, janela de 72 horas destacada
[02:15] To be clear, the logic of this specific campaign I would consider under the hammer them strategy. Okay, some people are also referring to this now as a mini tornado. Uh, whatever you want to call it.
[02:26] Okay, I export the list of webinar registrants the day of the webinar. I dynamically have it updating from the CRM. Meaning most CRM will have this functionality to be clear, but like in the marketing automation, if you have a function like Active Campaign or HubSpot or, you know, one of the other CRM that does this, you want to take the registrance as they come in and upload them dynamically to a customer list.
[02:50] That way, it's adding them in real time as they continue to come in the day of. Um to be clear, after I get that webinar registrant list uploaded and you know it's successfully populated and ready to go, we load it up into a campaign that is optimized for engagement.
[03:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Campaign Setup - Video Views Optimization"
NÚMEROS: 24 ad sets, 24 vídeos, 15 segundos, 3 segundos, 365 dias
VISUAL: Estrutura de campaign com múltiplos ad sets, ícone de visualização de vídeo, exclusões de audiência representadas
[03:12] On the ad set level, I have the ad sets optimized for video views and maximized throughplay views, which mean which means that we're optimizing for 15-second video views ideally.
[03:24] Um, to be clear, each ad set contains one piece of content and all the adsets are the exact same in terms of the targeting. The only difference is is each adset has the exclusion of a 3-second video view audience for the video within that particular ad set.
[03:42] So, long story short, in this particular case, we've got 24 total adsets. Therefore, we've got 24 total videos. And once again, the only variance between these adsets is on the adset level, we are excluding the video that is within that adset. 3 second video of you audience for a 365-day duration.
[04:04] To be clear, placement wise, if you are not cross-posting your reels to Facebook, then this placement is exclusive to Instagram. If you are cross-posting your reels to Facebook, then you can also run the Facebook placement.
[04:18] Does anybody have questions on that so far before I go through the technicals of the content?
[04:22] All right, cool. Let's go through these.

[06:48] "How much money would I have to give you for you to go back to taking care of executives instead of goats? No amount. No amount."

If you're forced to get out and make a living, sometimes you don't get the choice to do what you're passionate about. But if you can, you should do what you're passionate about, then it doesn't feel like work."

[08:14] 📊 CONTEÚDO DE VÍDEO 2 ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Entrepreneurship Reality Check"
NÚMEROS: Nenhum específico
VISUAL: Cody falando sobre dificuldades, negócios com problemas de caixa, funcionário-chave saindo, múltiplos desafios simultâneos
[08:16] [Vídeo mostrando]: "If you choose entrepreneurship, you did not ask for easy. Anybody who told you this is going to be easy live, but you did ask for it to be worth it. And I do think that this game of entrepreneurship is worth it."
[08:32] "I had a rough week where one of my businesses is super cash trapped. Another business thinks they can do a huge deal. They can't. they're not going to be able to get the deal done. I have another business that has with a key employee leaving and all that is happening this week"
[08:48] "and there's this wild thing about entrepreneurship where I think that the universe kind of asks you sometimes if you want a thing you tell it and then the universe goes and it just kind of kicks you a few times while you're down and you have to while you're getting kicked go no I'm sure I'm sure I still want it"
[09:08] "and so I bring this up because while I stand on stage with a big portfolio and all these companies if you think that I'm not getting kicked in the proverbial daily you're out of your mind. And so while this week may be awful, it doesn't mean that it's not going to open up for something pretty incredible. If you chose entrepreneurship, you..."
[09:28] Great. Another great piece of content there. This one specifically deals with the framing around, look, this isn't going to be easy, right?
[09:36] Ask yourself this for whatever service you're selling. Do you want people to buy it and just expect like an easy transition to whatever result or outcome that they want? if they have to put in work themselves.
[09:48] Like let's say you're selling a course as an example or like let's say you're selling agency services where the client has to do some work as well or you know whatever it is that you're selling.
[09:58] To be clear, being able to frame people ahead of time that you know there's there's going to be hard times like it's going to be times where you really got to push through and you still got to prevail, but it's going to be worth it. Makes a ton of sense to put as a framing piece of content in front of somebody during the buying cycle.
[10:16] That way they come through with more realistic expectations.

[10:54] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Polarizing Content Warning"
NÚMEROS: 80,000-100,000 seguidores perdidos
VISUAL: Tópicos polarizantes listados: política, religião, Trump, símbolos de divisão, espectro de reações
[10:56] So you you want to make sure you understand like the polarizing spectrum that exists uh you want to try to steer clear of the things that when somebody hears just immediately snaps them into a perspective of like okay I like that or like I hate that.
[11:12] Uh because that can once again get somebody to immediately just like disassociate with you completely and immediately go into the bucket of like no I don't fuck with this person anymore because they say that does that make sense?
[11:24] Like as an example, like I could be like carnivore or vegan, you know, and and that's not incredibly like instant polarizing, but if I was like if I pulled down my shirt and I had like a Trump tattoo right here, you know what I mean? Can you see how immediately that's like highly different?
[11:40] You have like a reactive thought to it, you know? Or if I had like a fucking Kamala tattoo on my on my fucking forearm, you know what I mean?
[11:48] uh even if I just express that I have like a radical belief about like dude some people as an example like they they hear that somebody's a particular religion you know and they just think a whole bunch of like specific thoughts immediately we want to try to steer clear of those
[12:04] not necessarily in the attempt to like I I want to be clear when I say this uh the belief that you likely internalize of you know I don't give a fuck what other people think like that's great as a self-preservation belief
[12:18] however when it comes comes to like content distribution and what we're attempting to do with the content. We absolutely want to give a fuck what the other person thinks, you know? That's the whole point of it.
[12:30] We're trying to persuade and and manipulate how they think to positively benefit them increasing their odds of buying from us or spending more money with us faster. You see what I'm saying?
[12:42] So, we we do give a fuck about what people think when it comes to content distribution. Okay?
[12:48] 📊 CONTEÚDO DE VÍDEO 3 ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Fear and Courage"
NÚMEROS: Nenhum específico
VISUAL: Cody discutindo medo, coragem, analogia de levantamento de peso, crescimento pessoal
[12:50] [Vídeo mostrando]: "Everyone has fear. There's not a person that doesn't have fear. Courage and bravery. Even in the face of fear, I choose to do the thing because it's hard. I choose to do the thing because it's better for somebody else."
[13:06] "And the byproduct of getting through that to make it super simple, I'm trying to like PR on a lift. Yeah, I'm fearful I'm not going to be able to lift it, but I lift it. The byproduct is I'm stronger after."
[13:20] "I know that's like a super oversimplication of it. But in the human condition, if I'm fearful to do this act, this hard thing, the selfless thing, this thing that is that that might hurt me, that is suffering. But on the other side of it is a better version of me, cool. I'm down for it. I'm here for that. Everyone has fear."
[13:42] Yeah. I hope you guys understand how obvious that one is. Uh to be clear, that's an example of an indirect piece of content that that's going to come up like that type of thought's going to come up in the buying cycle.
[13:54] You know, like I said, we're trying to transition people into buying businesses. like it's a there's a relative, you know, perceptually large financial risk to that. There's the time risk. Uh, you know, there's the emotional draw that comes from that type of risk.
[14:10] Uh, people, especially as they age, take less big risks because they have a lot more responsibilities. Um, to be clear, all that's going to come up in the buying cycle.
[14:20] Just ask yourself this. Does that video successfully plant the idea or help the person frame an opportunity as worth doing even if fear is present? That's what we're attempting to do with that piece of content when we use it.
[14:34] 📊 CONTEÚDO DE VÍDEO 4 ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Baby Boomer Business Opportunity"
NÚMEROS: $84 trilhões, 18 milhões de negócios, 25 anos
VISUAL: Números massivos, transferência de riqueza, gráficos de oportunidade, baby boomers se aposentando
[14:36] [Vídeo mostrando]: "$84 trillion, that is how much money is going to transfer from boomers to us, are about to retire. Why would they just pass on the family business? Well, their millennial kids don't want them."
[14:52] "So, we're going to have 18 million businesses over the next 25 years that unless they find new owners, those bad boys are going to zero, totally disappear."
[15:04] "But we're starting to see four problems playing out. The first one is a lot of the businesses are a mess. They can clean it up. The second is the owners have no clue how to list the business or find the right buyer. The third is they want too much money. And the final one is the buyers have no idea how to find and buy the good boomer businesses."
[15:24] "That's a lot of problems. So, what's the solution? I think we're building it. Comment early to be the first to hear about it."
[15:32] So, this is a classic central framing belief that we like to plant for the opportunity. Uh, essentially when somebody thinks, okay, I got to buy a business. Like, there's a lot of people out there who don't even think that there's enough businesses that you could even buy.
[15:48] Like, when you ask yourself a question as an example, because most of you, I'm sure, haven't bought businesses. Like, put in the chat real quick. How many businesses at one time do you think are available to purchase?
[16:02] Like take a moment once again, put in the chat at any given time. How many businesses do you think are available to purchase just in the US?
[16:12] Whatever you think to be clear, not I'm not even naming a specific location. Just wherever you would want to buy a business, I think that'd be appropriate. How many businesses do you guys think are available to purchase at a given time?
[16:24] Tom Elliot, where you at? Ash, what's your answers? Put it in the chat.
[16:30] So, Drew says probably millions. Gabe says around 700K. Adam says 500K. Ash says 500K. Tom, you got an answer? Elliot, you got an answer? Tom says not that many.

VISUAL: Gráfico mostrando variância nas crenças da audiência sobre disponibilidade de negócios
[16:46] Yeah. So, as an example, like look at the variance in your guys' answers. So, for somebody like Tom that says not that many, when I can get a few videos in front of Tom as an example that specifically talk about the fact that all these baby boomers have big businesses
[17:02] like what's the what's the belief about baby boomers? Like, think about this real quick. Like what's the propaganda already in your head about baby boomers?
[17:12] Like I think a lot of stuff about like lead poisoning and you know that they're pretty like selfish people. But another thing I think is like dude these people have a fuckload of wealth.
[17:24] Like I have seen a lot of propaganda about the fact that baby boomers have like had one of the greatest generational opportunities in the history of like life to get wealthy.
[17:36] Um, I've heard and seen like graphs, you know, that demonstrate the amount of wealth that baby boomers have versus like all the other generations collectively are like, you know, a fourth of it.
[17:48] Uh, you know, my point is is like there's already some thoughts in my head about how boomers have a lot of money. So, if I get the opportunity to also connect the dots that like, oh shit yeah, like they run businesses and they're all going to die sooner, just want to retire
[18:04] like, yeah, they're going to want to sell their businesses for a lump sum or something or like how like it just plants the belief and the idea that there are likely more businesses than what you'd consider available to purchase at a given time. And it also sells you on like why.
[18:22] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Power of WHY"
NÚMEROS: 2017, Las Vegas, Mandalay Bay
VISUAL: Importância do "porquê" na narrativa, exemplo de tiroteio em Vegas, loops abertos vs fechados
[18:24] Like, it's very important. Humans require a why. I'll give you a great example. Does anybody remember in 2017 uh there was a crazy shooting that happened in Las Vegas in the Mandalay Bay Hotel there was a shooter on like I think it was like the 60s something floor.
[18:42] Uh he had like dude like infinite guns. He had like hundreds of guns and he there was a country concert and he just literally open fired on this concert and I remember one night I was sitting in in Los Angeles and dude I like saw these videos coming out of this fucking happening in real time
[19:00] and I was like holy shit like why is this guy doing this? There was never a why that got released. So whenever there's not a why, there's like a rampid amount of conspiracies that start to come out because humans have to fill the void for why something happens.
[19:18] Without a why, there's not a lot of conviction that can occur because it's like an open loop. We have to close the loop for people with a why.
[19:28] So when we tell them in this case like look, we want to teach you how to buy businesses. Well, there's a lot of open loops that happen immediately in terms of like why, you know?
[19:40] So, one of the big whys is well, in terms of opportunities, like all these boomers are going to retire. They need somebody to come in and run their businesses like they want to sell. They have the reason to sell.
[19:54] Now, to be clear, there's other beliefs that hopefully we can see in this content here today where she talks about seller financing as an example where she where she says, "Look, you don't even have to come out of pocket a lot of money."
[20:08] "You know, believe it or not, like a lot of these people, they're going to be willing to let you run the business for like a very little down payment, maybe nothing, and profit share with you because simply put, they don't want to do it themselves anymore, but they don't want to give up the cash flow because now they want to retire."
[20:26] You know, that's an incredible why to tie into the baby boomer logic that we just planted as a belief.
[20:34] So, a lot of people sometimes think in content that you need to have like a fully well-rounded immediate answer for people. It's okay to piece that content out in a few different pieces of content.
[20:46] So like in the baby boomer one, I'm just introducing like the opportunity in a sense. In the seller financing, I need to tie back into the baby boomer logic of like, well, they want to retire, but they don't want to give up the cash flow.
[21:00] They don't just want to live off a lump sum forever, you know? They need money, too, to survive, you know? So anyway, that's another great piece of content. Let's see what this one is.
[21:12] 📊 CONTEÚDO DE VÍDEO 5 ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Passion vs Profits"
NÚMEROS: Nenhum específico
VISUAL: Discussão sobre seguir paixão vs ROI, analogia de academia, dor de curto prazo vs ganho de longo prazo
[21:14] [Vídeo mostrando]: "What are your thoughts on doing work that you love and doing work that you enjoy? I think we were sold a bag of lies when people told us to follow our passion and it will lead to profits."
[21:28] "Are you chasing shortterm joy and not realizing that short-term joy leads to long-term pain? And I think most of us have been sold falsities lately. We've got so much hustle culture back in the day that we like reacted against it and now we think that we can go around float around do whatever we want. We don't have any repercussions."
[21:48] "I am saying if you get to choose between something that is hard but has a high ROI on the thing you care about a high potential for what you really care about in life do it even if it hurts. Nobody likes going to the gym to start but then you actually end up loving the game."
[22:06] Yeah, tremendous piece of content. Tremendous. To be clear in terms of the indirect framing like she's going to tell people in some of these videos that we'll see here soon.
[22:18] uh look, you need to buy like a rehab business or you need to buy like a like a plumbing business or an HVAC business or like you know, she's really big into bluecollar businesses specifically and what she refers to as boring businesses.
[22:34] So, as an example, if you saw a piece of content, whether it came before or after that tells you, hey, I need you to go buy an AC company, an HVAC company, like you're probably thinking to yourself, oh, I'm not going to have a lot of joy doing that.
[22:50] Like, I'm not going to have a lot of fun doing that. Like, that doesn't sound like I'm going to be passionate about it. And like that specific piece of content and telling people like that's bullshit to think that you need to be having fun, you know, while you're making a fuckload of money.
[23:06] Like you you get the money and then you go have fun after. You know, that type of belief management is without without exaggerating. That's an incredible example of an objection that might not be spoken but will hold somebody back.
[23:22] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Subconscious Objections"
NÚMEROS: Nenhum
VISUAL: Objeções faladas vs não faladas, iceberg com objeções visíveis acima da água e subconscientes abaixo
[23:24] You know, think about this very importantly, okay? Put a lot of emphasis on this for yourself. You hear objections like, "Oh, I don't have the money." Or like, "I need to talk to somebody." Like just the classic little objections.
[23:38] What you don't hear are the things that the person might not even be self-aware of that hold them back on the subconscious level. Like that'd be a great example of that.
[23:50] Do you think on a sales call that we would hear, "Yeah, well, I don't really want to buy a fucking AC company. I don't think that's going to be a lot of fun." Like that's likely something somebody's not necessarily going to be super self-aware of.
[24:04] They might voice that objection as I don't really know if this is for me. they they genuinely might not have the conscious awareness to consider why they don't want to do it.
[24:16] When we plant that kind of belief and we just make them that's all that's all planting beliefs is guys is just introducing the thought so they can think with it. Okay?
[24:28] People don't know what they don't know. We can't expect them to have like thoughts about things. Not everybody takes time to critically think about each thing that's going to hold them back or make them do something.
[24:42] When we introduce thoughts at the time that a decision arises, we now know that there's thoughts in that person's head that we planted that they might used to think with. Do you get the importance of it?
[24:56] 📊 PERGUNTA DA AUDIÊNCIA ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Timing Question - Before or After Direct Response"
NÚMEROS: 2 meses, 72 horas
VISUAL: Timeline de funil, framing antes vs depois de ads diretos
[24:58] [Pergunta do participante]: "in this particular in this particular funnel, are you framing them before you hit them with direct ads like the harvester or are you doing it like the uh after they've seen the direct response ad?"
[25:12] No. So in this case, this is this is uh they've seen direct response ads and registered for a webinar and then we are we hammered them.
[25:24] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Campaign Frequency Statistics"
NÚMEROS: Frequência 11, 72 horas, 24 peças de conteúdo
VISUAL: Dashboard do Facebook Ads mostrando frequência 11.2 em 3 dias, múltiplos ad sets
[25:26] To be clear, if you actually check the uh the frequency statistics on this particular campaign that we're referring to, uh we have a frequency of 11 within uh 72 hours. See down here?
[25:40] So within three days from the day of the webinar to two days after the webinar, we managed to hit people 11 times out of 24 pieces of content.
[25:52] [Pergunta]: "Question. If you're doing a DM or you're doing uh ad to DM for setter, then sales call. Generally what I do is I put like a 30-day cycle of anybody that's DM'd me in the last 30 days. Would that be a pretty good idea? because that's what I've kind of been doing as like a content cycle bin."
[26:12] Well, so it depends. So, in this case, you know, one of the questions that nobody's asked yet is, "Well, Jeremy, why 72 hours? Like, why did you only do that 72 hours?"
[26:24] And I did that 72 hours because that's when people have the highest probability to make a decision. So, to be clear, I want the highest concentration of content. Meaning in terms of the frequency, I want the frequency to be the absolute highest at key decision-making moments in my buying cycle.
[26:44] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "72-Hour Decision Window"
NÚMEROS: 72 horas, 4 decisões-chave
VISUAL: Timeline mostrando: registro webinar → assistir webinar → agendar call → comparecer call, tudo em 72h
[26:46] So this in this case, showing up to the webinar and then during the webinar and then booking a call and then showing up to that call. Those all happen typically within 72 hours from the day of the webinar to two days after the webinar. Those four key decisions all happen right then and there. So I'm hammering people during that window.
[27:08] Now, I still have content running towards these people just like you do over a broader duration of time such as a 14 or 30 day cycle. I like to select a rolling amount of time for whatever the average sales cycle is of a lead.
[27:24] So, in your in your case, as an example, if it takes somebody who DMs uh three weeks on average to end up purchasing, and that's like your historical sales cycle time frame, that's where I would want my highest frequency to occur is over an entire 3-week duration.
[27:42] if on average and this and this varies depending on the business uh like we had Adrian on the call here today who represents James uh in James' business a majority of their leads like it seemed like 90% plus were buying within a week.
[27:58] So their their rolling window for when that audience of people who are booking a call should be dynamically updating and then dropping those people out is essentially a week. That's when they should seek their highest frequency. Does that make sense?
[28:14] Yeah, makes 100% sense. Um, quick question. So, obviously when you're doing a webinar, it's super easy to retract that person because obviously it has a pixel on the website, right?
[28:26] If you're doing a D, if you're doing a setter to DM, obviously, and then the sales call, obviously we get their information. Is there any way to put them in a 72-hour cycle just from getting their information from a sales call?

[34:46] 📊 CONTEÚDO DE VÍDEO 6 ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Why Restaurants Fail"
NÚMEROS: $800,000 média de venda de pequenos negócios, $198,000 média de restaurantes, 60% falham no primeiro ano, 80% falham em 4 anos, 3-5% margens, 22.7 anos, $95,000-$2 milhões custo de construção

[34:48] [Vídeo mostrando]: "Ain't for me. They're incredible restaurant groups and people who make millions doing it. You know, you got Nou, Fox Restaurant, Concepts, Mafukco, etc. But the truth is restaurants are incredibly complex as a business."
[35:04] "The average small business in the US sells for around $800,000. The average restaurant goes for $198,000. Why? Because 60% fail in the first year and 80% fail after four."
[35:20] "Second is margins. On average, restaurants can expect net margins of 3 to 5%. You're in trouble if you make a few mistakes. A few people don't show up, food goes sideways, you're out of business."
[35:36] "Third, competition. This is what's called a red ocean market. A red ocean is where you have so much competition, you can't throw a rock without hitting another. Eating at every restaurant in New York City, for example, it would take you 22.7 years."
[35:54] "The fourth reason I don't like them is procurement, forecasting demand, storage, waste, staff, managing off peak hours, pricing, calculating gross profit of each food item. Let's take just spoilage. You not only have to buy up inventory intelligently, but you have to hope that you are so good at guessing if the fickle crowds will eat your 3-day old shrimp before they go bad."
[36:18] "They're really expensive restaurants to build out cost anywhere from 95,000 to 2 million. And then you got to deal with all of your employees in the back doing dirty deeds. I don't care if you're a foodie or your buds tell you that you should sell that perfect cheeseburger that you make."
[36:34] "Don't do it unless they're giving you the startup cash. Restaurants are for humans that simply cannot exist without running one in my opinion. So go eat there. Go frequent them. But, you know, don't do deals before you leave."

[51:08] "which is a US labor law that protects employees by requiring most employers with over a 100 employees to provide a 60 calendar day heads up of planned closings and mass layoffs."
[51:22] "Although most people don't know about it, it's actually as simple as just googling warm act plus your state and you'll find a government website that will show you a full list of places employees are getting laid off. [54:22] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Cause & Effect Content Strategy"
NÚMEROS: Nenhum específico
VISUAL: Pessoas com empregos tradicionais olhando para baixo para criadores de conteúdo, mas criadores tendo mais sucesso financeiro
[54:24] So, like people with legitimate jobs, uh, like picture like an engineer or like a developer, like you know, whatever the fuck you do, and you're considering transitioning to buying a business.
[54:38] I I I say this all the time. If you can find an example of what people who are your ideal demographic think is like like they're better than or above, you know, and that person's doing far better than them financially or in life in general. That is a huge motivator for change for an individual who has like the traditional path.
[54:58] So keep in mind like we're we're essentially selling people on buying a business. A lot of these people come from traditional paths where they're like I say like a software engineer, an architect, a developer, a regular engineer, whatever. All kinds of crazy shit
[55:14] My point being is like those individuals look down on content creators. Like they think there's in they think there's no security to it, you know? They think there's no there's no health benefits, there's no free food, yada yada.
[55:28] So they like look down on it and they they also just view it as an untraditional path. So they don't view it as something that u is like better than them. They view it as like worse than them.
[55:42] And when they hear that that type of individual is doing financially and just in life better than them, it makes them question everything about what they've done. You know, like as an example, like the classic, well, I didn't go to college. Like look how much better I'm doing than the people who went to college.
[56:00] Look at all you guys. Like did you guys go to college? You know, I'm sure some of you did to be fair, but you know, a lot of you probably didn't. It's like my point being when you have that kind of story go out there of like, yeah, you know, I didn't go to school.
[56:16] And you're selling to people who did go to school and took the traditional path, that type of content makes them think like, holy shit dude. Like, if that guy can do it, like, I'm I'm way better than that guy. I'm smarter than that guy. I went to school, you know, that guy didn't. Like, are you kidding me?
[56:34] Uh, just because you didn't go to college doesn't make them think like you're smarter than them because you're making more money. It actually makes them think, "Dude, I'm smarter than that person who's doing better than me. So, if they can do it, and I think they're dumber than me, I could crush them." Does that make sense?
[56:52] That's that that's what we call a piece of content that doesn't directly say that, but that's what they think is a result of saying that. Does that make sense, dude? Baller piece of content. That's what I call a cause and effect piece of content. You get what I'm saying?
[57:08] Like I constantly think this as an example about Logan Paul. I think Logan Paul is a fucking I think I'm like, "Oh my god." And we're from the same place. So I constantly look at Logan Paul and I think to myself, dude, if his ass can do it, you know what I mean?
[57:26] Like there's no way there's no way I can't get infinitely richer if Logan Paul is still today richer than me. You know what I mean? That's an example of what I'm talking about is you want to give people examples of something like that if if once again that's what you're selling to.
[57:44] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Cause & Effect Example - Dentistry"
NÚMEROS: $50,000 smile makeover, $100,000
VISUAL: Antes e depois extremo (meth mouth vs smile perfeito), pessoa com problema menor vendo resultado maior
[57:46] Like I said, the premise of this piece of content just so you can relate it to anything that you want to do is cause and effect. Okay?
[57:56] So like as an example with uh with Ash, like he sells to dentists and he's, you know, probable to start selling to cosmetic dentists as well who like do makeover smiles.
[58:08] Imagine somebody has like super fucked up teeth, like teeth that are like not even forward. They're like completely sideways. They're like missing a tooth. And there's like a piece of before content and after content.
[58:22] The person with like a slightly fucked up smile. And I just mean like it's a little little yellow. You know what I mean? And there's like one tiny little crooked tooth. Nothing noticeable. They barely see it themselves. They notice it because they look at their smile in the mirror every day.
[58:38] It's like the person who has the barely fucked up smile sees the guy with like meth mouth who comes from the before and after. They think to themselves, "Dude, holy shit if that guy can do that smile to that guy, like what can they do to me? Like, I'm in a way better condition. My shit's probably going to be like,"
[58:58] and if the guy, imagine this, right? On top of that, imagine if the guy with the meth mouth that gets the beautiful smile makeover says, "Yeah, I had barely any recovery time. This was actually a fucking breeze. It took like no time at all. I was actually shocked at how fast it was, you know?
[59:16] Then once again, the person with like the barely fucked up smiles thinks to themselves, "Oh my god, if that guy's saying that, like, dude, this shit's probably going to take like an hour." You know what I mean?
[59:28] Like there's a lot of assumptions that come from cause and effect content that benefit your buying cycle when done successfully. Does that make sense, everybody?
[59:40] Okay.
[59:42] [Pergunta]: "What? Sorry. What would be an example of cause and effect content there?"
[59:48] I just You're gonna have to watch your recording on that.
[59:52] Yeah. Okay.
[59:54] Yeah, okay, so to be clear, let's go through some more.
[60:00] 📊 CONTEÚDO DE VÍDEO 10 ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "College Education ROI Reality"
NÚMEROS: Nenhum específico
VISUAL: Gráfico mostrando crescimento astronômico do custo da educação superior vs estagnação dos retornos, dívida estudantil recorde
[60:02] [Vídeo mostrando]: "I think we were lied to as a generation. We were told, "Hey, just get good grades, get into a good college, don't worry about student loans. You'll make it up over time, then go get a good job and you're going to be okay." And it was a fucking lie."
[60:20] "What you see here, fancy words, but the diminishing financial return of higher education. So we've had astronomical growth in the cost of higher education and flatlining growth in the uh return of higher education."
[60:36] "The truth of the matter is is that we should have looked at the numbers and the numbers say that we are making other people rich namely institutions and their investors and we are by no means better off as a generation from having the highest level of student debt of all time and the highest level of education of all time."
[60:56] "And increasingly, higher education does not now always lead to a higher income, which is what they say college will help you do. And so, if you want to go to college just to have fun, that's awesome. But realize that college doesn't seem to actually increase your earnings potential over time."
[61:14] Okay. So, as I mentioned before, like she sells to a demographic that's typically gone to college and has like some type of professional job. So that's a piece of content that you're going to see as like a recurring, you know, hit it from different angles piece of content where that's the reality of the situation.
[61:32] Like people who went to school, they're not more financially well off and and they live that reality. So it's not necessarily a piece of content that's like a shock. It's more so like something that somebody sees who's working a job and they say to themselves like, "Fuck, dude. Yeah, that's right."
[61:50] You know, like I I'm really not doing much better than everybody else. And at the same time, they also think in terms of group think. They think to themselves, damn, like everybody else is going through it, too.
[62:04] And then to be clear, there's one additional thought that comes kind of as a question to that. Can anybody guess what that question would be?
[62:14] You're in college, you went, you got out, you got a job, you're not as well paid as what you ever thought you'd be. You then realize nobody else is is getting well paid after they went to college. It's kind of a situation that everybody else is in. What's the question that gets prompted in your head after you after you conclude to that?

[62:40] Like what do I do then? Like what the fuck do I do? Right? Isn't that kind of what comes up?

[67:26] And uh remember, you you you have to tie in a few different things here to be able to like, you know, work work all this together, which is you always want to think with once again like who's your buyer, okay? You're making content that persuades the buyer. That's it.
[67:44] Like you're not making content for whatever else you think. [68:18] And like I said, these can be answered in direct and indirect ways. Uh this I mean, there's nothing better. Like it's just silly not to do these things. It's so cost-effective. It makes so much more money.
[68:32] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Indirect Causation Warning"
NÚMEROS: $175,500 pagamento, 3 vídeos, 80,000-100,000 seguidores
VISUAL: Correlação indireta, sem atribuição direta possível, mas resultados óbvios
[68:34] But I want to be clear when I say this. It's an indirect causation. Okay? As an example, like you guys saw me post in the inner circle chat that I had that prop firm pay me $175,500. Okay? and they had me do three different videos.
[68:54] Okay, Shawn, the guy who introduced me to the opportunity at the same time got requested to make three videos. However, Shawn is anonymous. Shawn requests that his name doesn't get posted in the leaderboard of people who get paid the most. Shawn does not post in their Facebook group. Okay?
[69:12] I I am a positive propaganda machine for that company. I go in their Facebook group and I fucking spite spectics into the ground. I fucking argue with people. I tell them they're fucking Like I make posts saying, "Look, I just got approved for this. I just got approved for this."
[69:30] So the other day I make a post and I say, "Hey, like I finally made it to the fourth month where I have uncapped earning potential. I requested 1755 and they requested I make three videos." I'm not skeptical at all. You guys would probably be tweaking out. I'm chilling. I'm going to do what they ask. I trust them. I'll keep y'all updated, but like it'll be fine.
[69:52] You know, and obviously I get a bunch of people in the comments that are like, "Hey, congratulations." I get a bunch of other people that are like, "Oh, you're fucked." Like, you know, who the fuck makes you film videos before you get paid what you earn? You know, and I'm going back and forth with those motherfuckers
[70:08] And I'm like, "No, dude." Like they're trying to prevent scammers. Like this makes perfect sense. $175,500 in a month. Like yeah, it makes I'm going to help them whenever I can when they request it from me.
[70:24] Now, to be clear, when I submit my first three videos last Friday, I get an immediate response like within two hours. That's like, "Hey, Jeremy, sorry, bro. These won't work. You didn't show the executions tab with the timestamps in the name. Uh, I'm sorry." you know, but like you're gonna have to refilm them again.
[70:44] And I was like, fuck you. Like that sucks. But it is what it is. Like I'm like, all right, I'll do it. No problem.
[70:52] Shawn didn't get a response once. Sean still hasn't heard from these people. Okay.
[70:58] I then go in on Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday of this week. I filmed three videos. I send them on Wednesday. I get a response again within two, three hours that's like, "All right, cool, man. These ones work. We'll review them and we'll get back to you."
[71:14] I woke up on Thursday to that email that hit at midnight that said, "Hey, congratulations. You're paid." You know, like everything's approved. And and I hit up Sean. I'm like, "Yo, look at this. Like, I already got approved." I'm like, "Have you heard from him yet?" Sean goes, "No, I haven't heard from them at all. Like, I haven't heard anything."

[71:36] Okay, here's my point. I knew that if I went into that group where all the people who are responsible for my fucking payouts, from the executives to the managers to the payout support team to the random customer service reps, I know I'll have visibility
[71:54] and I know that I can press these people to successfully demonstrate like, dude, I'm one of the only people, if not the only person that literally stands up for this company and says You can trust these motherfuckers dude.
[72:08] Like, yeah, they're slow because they operate at a scale that's bigger than every other fucking company existing combined. Like, if you're skeptical, you're fucking is pretty much what I say, you know? Like, trust the process, shut the fuck up, do what they ask, follow the rules, you know?

NÚMEROS: $170,000 diferença diária de lucro, menos registrantes, mesmas ofertas
VISUAL: Impossibilidade de correlação direta, mas diferença óbvia e mensurável nos resultados
[74:14] It's going to be the same thing with your propaganda content machines. You will never be able to point your finger at it and say well I'll give you an example like I have I'm not going to be able to share these statistics just because these are Cody's company statistics to be clear
[74:30] but I I will share this. Okay. So, without sharing like the whole Excel sheet, okay, I'll read a few things off to you.

[75:20] There's no difference in content. There's no difference in what's talked about in the webinar. There's no difference in the opt-in page. There's technically a slight difference down in total registrance.
[75:34] Why has there been 170,000 lift in the total quantity of revenue per day that comes from the webinar? Well, I can't definitively say it's the content, but it sure looks like it is. Huh?
[75:50] That's what I'm talking about. You'll never be able to definitively say this is a result of that fucking content. Okay? That's what I'm trying to tell you with the example of the prop firm. That's what I'm trying to tell you the example of the $170,000 daily lifts.
[76:08] But my point being that is the effect of content. I know that. You'll know that. You'll see it if you don't do it. Okay? But to be clear, you'll never be able to have a direct correlation.
[76:22] So that's the most important part of the whole content machine. You'll never be able to set the expectation that it's a trackable difference, but you'll see a substantial difference right after you do it. Does that make sense?
[76:36] Yes.

[78:00] All right. What?
📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
10-15,000 registrantes de webinar mensalmente
$80,000 investimento mensal em ads para webinar
72 horas de janela de targeting intensivo
24 ad sets com 24 vídeos únicos
Frequência 11.2 em 72 horas (11 vídeos únicos vistos)
Frequência 10-15 em campanha de 30 dias
Otimização para 15 segundos de visualização
Exclusão de 3 segundos de visualização por 365 dias
$10,000 oferta de curso/comunidade
$175,500 pagamento de prop firm
$170,000 diferença diária de lucro nos últimos 2 webinars
$84 trilhões em transferência de riqueza de baby boomers
18 milhões de negócios disponíveis em 25 anos
$800,000 valor médio de pequeno negócio
$198,000 valor médio de restaurante
60% de restaurantes falham no primeiro ano
80% falham em 4 anos
3-5% margens líquidas em restaurantes
22.7 anos para comer em todos restaurantes de NYC
$95,000-$2 milhões custo de construção de restaurante
$100,000/ano vendendo cabras
$50,000 smile makeover
$35,000 exemplo de cliente dental
9 milhões de inscritos YouTube (Joshua Weissman)
Centenas de milhares de cookbooks vendidos
100+ funcionários (WARN Act requirement)
60 dias de aviso de demissão (WARN Act)
80,000-100,000 seguidores perdidos por conteúdo político
2017 Las Vegas shooting (exemplo de "why")
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Hammer Them Strategy / Mini Tornado: Concentração máxima de conteúdo em janela de decisão chave
72-Hour Intensive Window: Do dia do webinar até 2 dias depois
4 Key Decisions: Registro → Assistir webinar → Agendar call → Comparecer call
4 Content Types Framework: Framing, Objection Handling, Get to Know You, Belief Management
Dynamic Customer Lists: Atualização em tempo real via CRM
Video View Optimization: Maximized throughplay views (15 segundos)
3-Second Exclusion Rule: Após 3 segundos de visualização, nunca mais ver aquele vídeo
24 Ad Set Structure: Um vídeo por ad set, targeting idêntico, exclusão única
Multi-Campaign Approach: 72h intensivo + 30 dias mais amplo
Frequency Targeting: Alta concentração em momentos-chave de decisão
Rolling Window Strategy: Baseado no ciclo de vendas médio
Conversion API + Pixel: Rastreamento e atualização de audiências
Marketing Automation: Zapier ou CRM nativo para atualização dinâmica
Placement Strategy: Instagram exclusivo ou cross-posting com Facebook
Content Breakout Strategy: Dividir vídeos gerais em específicos detalhados
Cause & Effect Content: Prompting auto-gerado de perguntas e conclusões
Polarizing Content Goal: Forçar escolha de lado da cerca (vendido ou não)
Subconscious Objection Handling: Abordar objeções não faladas
Transition Content: Dar confiança para grandes mudanças
Why Provision: Sempre fornecer razões e contexto
Thought Provision: Dar às pessoas pensamentos para pensar com
Indirect Framing: Plantar crenças sem vender diretamente
Propaganda Machine: Defesa ativa e visível da marca/produto
ESTRUTURA:
Client Profile: Cody Sanchez - Ex-Goldman Sachs, ensina compra de negócios
Target Demographic: Profissionais bem-pagos (tech, engenharia, etc.), pessoas com empregos estáveis, graduados universitários, salários de 6 figuras
Main Objections: "Já tenho um bom emprego", "Não estou sentindo dor", "Não sei se é para mim", "Não vou me divertir"
Content Posting: Tudo em Reels (Instagram/Facebook)
Content Selection Process: Conversar com sales team, identificar objeções diretas, pensar em objeções indiretas/subconscientes
Objection Evolution: Objeções ficam mais sofisticadas com bom conteúdo
Avoid Polarizing Topics: Política, religião, tópicos instantaneamente divisivos
CRM Requirements: Active Campaign, HubSpot, Go High Level (com marketing automation)
Comment Analysis: Statements = crença adotada, Questions = não finalizado
Content Distribution Timeline: Registrantes veem ads diretos → Registram webinar → 72h de hammer → 30 dias de manutenção
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
$80,000/mês em ad spend para webinar
$10,000 oferta de produto
$170,000 aumento diário de lucro (últimos 2 webinars)
$175,500 pagamento único de prop firm
ROI indireto mas substancial
Sem atribuição direta possível
Causação indireta óbvia nos resultados
Lift significativo em conversão e revenue
Redução no tempo de ciclo de vendas
Aumento na confiança do comprador
Menos registrantes mas mais lucro (qualidade > quantidade)
DURAÇÃO: ~78 minutos FORMATO: Group coaching call / Screen share / Live Instagram content review
